coredata.dc:title: Impact of CB dispersion on the performance of lithium-ion battery cathodes
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Weber, Marcel
Gerstenberg, Jessica
Kwade, Arno
coredata.dc:description: Lithium-ion battery electrode performance is heavily dependent on the carbon binder domain. Both, experimental and simulation studies have affirmed its substantial impact on electrode properties, yet a precise quantification remains challenging. This study uses a co-rotating twin-screw extruder to adjust the carbon binder domain by varying mixing intensity. Employing the Carbon Black Dispersion Index (DICB), we quantitatively evaluate changes in carbon black (CB) particle size and their impact on electrodes and cells. The results highlight the crucial role of CB dispersion in shaping the carbon binder domain. The correlation of the DICB with electrode properties reveals an optimal dispersion intensity range, especially for rate capability and ionic resistance. Customizing the dispersion process using DICB led to a remarkable 26 % enhancement in specific discharge capacity at high C-rates. This research emphasizes DICB's significance in optimizing electrode performance and offers a novel approach to tailor the dispersion process for more efficient lithium-ion battery electrodes. Such tailored approaches hold promise for creating greener energy storage solutions, with implications for industrial applications, process scaling, and quality control procedures.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Slurry mixing
Carbon black
lithium-ion battery
Quality control
Extrusion
Cell performance

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: quality assessment and development cycles in (a) state of the art production and (b) utilizing the DICB.
Experimental overview of process parameters and characterization methods.
CB particle size distributions for different solids contents cm,kn.
Impact of increasing specific energy input Em on (a) the CB dispersion and slurry viscosity as well as (b) complex rheology.

Electrical and mechanical properties in relation to the CB structure represented by the DICB (a) resistivity and adhesion strength measured using a material testing machine Zwick Z020 (b) volume and interface resistance measured using a Hioki resistance measurement system RM2610.
Impact of DICB on the (a) pore size distribution of uncalendered electrodes and (b) theoretical porosity and inner CB porosity of uncalendered and calendered electrodes.

half cell data of a) cycling with varying CB dispersion progress b) impact of DICB on the rate capability including data of an additional trial in the estimated target range (suggested by the data fit), which is highlighted in orange.

head.ce:title: Impact of CB dispersion on the performance of lithium-ion battery cathodes

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Particle Technology, TU Braunschweig, Volkmaroder Str. 5, 38104 Braunschweig, Germany
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Particle Technology, TU Braunschweig, Volkmaroder Str. 5, 38104 Braunschweig

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Battery LabFactory Braunschweig, Langer Kamp 19, 38106, Braunschweig, Germany
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Battery LabFactory Braunschweig, Langer Kamp 19, 38106 Braunschweig

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Institute of Particle Technology, TU Braunschweig, Volkmaroder Str. 5, 38104 Braunschweig, Germany.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Lithium-ion battery electrode performance is heavily dependent on the carbon binder domain. Both, experimental and simulation studies have affirmed its substantial impact on electrode properties, yet a precise quantification remains challenging. This study uses a co-rotating twin-screw extruder to adjust the carbon binder domain by varying mixing intensity. Employing the Carbon Black Dispersion Index (DICB), we quantitatively evaluate changes in carbon black (CB) particle size and their impact on electrodes and cells. The results highlight the crucial role of CB dispersion in shaping the carbon binder domain. The correlation of the DICB with electrode properties reveals an optimal dispersion intensity range, especially for rate capability and ionic resistance. Customizing the dispersion process using DICB led to a remarkable 26 % enhancement in specific discharge capacity at high C-rates. This research emphasizes DICB's significance in optimizing electrode performance and offers a novel approach to tailor the dispersion process for more efficient lithium-ion battery electrodes. Such tailored approaches hold promise for creating greener energy storage solutions, with implications for industrial applications, process scaling, and quality control procedures.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The ongoing process of decarbonizing the transportation sector has positioned battery electric vehicles (BEVs) as the viable alternative to internal combustion engine vehicles (ICEVs). The focal point of BEVs lies in their battery modules, which are currently in the spotlight of research. State-of-the-art battery modules comprise lithium-ion batteries (LIBs). These LIBs consist of lithium-ion battery electrodes, comprising active material (AM), a conductive additive, and a binder (B). Particularly, in the case of the cathode, where the AM exhibits poor conductivity, the conductive additive plays a pivotal role in facilitating the electrical connection between particles and the current collector.

Commonly deployed conductive additives in state-of-the-art electrodes are carbon black (CB) and conductive graphite. In recent years, materials such as graphene and carbon nanotubes have gathered substantial interest in both research and industry [1–5]. Although all these materials offer commendable electrical conductivity, CB stands out as an exceptionally versatile additive due to its hierarchical particle structure. This structure provides a multitude of options for fine-tuning CB properties for the application in battery electrodes. However, effectively harnessing the high complexity of this material requires precise processing. Numerous research activities in the past have addressed the processing of LIB electrodes with the aim of enhancing their properties and electrochemical performance [6–9].

Ionic and electrical conductivity have been identified as key properties for LIB electrode performance [2,8,10]. Additionally, ensuring the mechanical integrity of the electrode is crucial to prevent defects during assembly. Those properties are closely linked to the porosity and microstructure of the electrode. Both of these attributes are affected by the interaction between CB and the binder, resulting in the formation of the Carbon Binder Domain (CBD). Besides material and formulation, the CBD is subject to various factors throughout the electrode manufacturing process.

As the initial step in a series of consecutive processes, the mixing process significantly impacts subsequent processes such as coating, drying, and calendering. In state-of-the-art electrode production, the mixing process must meet several requirements. Firstly, it must ensure proper wetting and an even distribution of raw materials on the current collector to guarantee optimal electrode performance. Moreover, for slot die coating, it is imperative to match a specific viscosity range to ensure homogenous coating and well-defined edges. Lastly, agglomerates, particularly in the case of CB, must be deagglomerated to prevent coating defects [11,12].

The structure of CB and the resulting CBD play a critical role in the functionality of the electrode [13], affecting mechanical and electrical integrity [2,10,14,15], pore structure, and subsequently ion and electron transport within the electrode [10,14,16]. Serveral research activities have addressed the mixing process (both wet and dry) in the past, investigating the impact of process design and properties [2,15,17,18].

Despite a wide range of devices and formulations used to study the mixing process, research consistently concurs that CB structure and CBD change significantly with varying process design and mixing intensity [10,15]. CB is renowned for its hierarchical structure, composed of primary particles, aggregates, and agglomerates [19]. The agglomerates of CB arise of multiple aggregates which are weakly bond by van der Waals forces and eventually selective solid bonds. During mixing, agglomerates are subjected to stress and fracturing with increasing stress intensity [14] and in certain cases, even fixation of CB on the AM surfaces may occur [2,15]. These structural changes in CB directly influence the initial formation of the CBD, thereby impacting slurry, electrode, and cell properties. Several research articles have attempted to characterize the relationship between CB structure, binder, and the resulting CBD by correlating these structural changes with physical and electrochemical properties [2,15,20,21]. A comprehensive review of the CBD has been provided by Entwistle et al. [9]. However, specifying the desired structure of the CBD remains challenging, primarily due to the intricate particulate interactions combined with the production processes. Different methods, including optical, physical, or electrochemical approaches, have been employed in research articles to quantify microstructure and CBD. Initial research in electrode processing utilized rheology measurements in conjunction with SEM imaging and porosimetry to evaluate the CBD [2,10,15]. Dreger et al. [22] later introduced a method for measuring CB particle size in electrode slurry using laser diffraction. This method has been utilized in various research articles on electrode processing and mixing [14,23,24]. Mayer et al. [17] introduced an innovative analysis method that describes the CB structure based on the inner CB porosity.

Besides porosity, the tortuosity and diffusion coefficient of lithium in the electrolyte are directly affected by the CBD and strongly influence charge transport in lithium-ion battery electrodes. Tortuosity, representing the elongation of the transport path, is correlated with the effective ionic conductivity within the electrode, which has been measured using various methods [25–31]. Ogihara et al. [28,29] and Landesfeind et al. [27] measured ionic resistance in a symmetrical cell configuration under blocking conditions using 0 % and 100 % state of charge or non-intercalating electrolyte salts. They applied a transmission line model (TLM) to the AC impedance data. If the electronic resistance in a battery electrode is negligible compared to the ionic resistance, and blocking conditions prevent charge transfer between solid and liquid phases, a simplified transmission line model can be applied [27]. Chi et al. [4] investigated the effect of graphene and CB on lithium-ion diffusion using electrochemical impedance spectroscopy. They found that the electrode with CB exhibited the highest ionic resistance, which they attributed to the internal pore structure. In contrast, graphene as a conductive additive offered lower ionic resistance but weaker C-rate capability. Combining both materials improved both ionic resistance and rate capability, a behavior attributed to changes in the CBD.

Especially with regard to reducing the scrap rate and in the context of smart manufacturing, early quantification of CB structure and resulting CBD is critical for maximizing and securing electrode performance. This publication leverages the Carbon Black Dispersion Index DICB [32] to offer novel insights into the impact of the mixing process on CB structure and resulting cathode characteristics. The DICB is applied to quantify the changes in CB structure due to varying mixing intensities in a twin-screw extrusion process. The resulting slurries are further processed into electrodes and characterized regarding their morphology and microstructure. The results emphasize the significance of the mixing process throughout electrode manufacturing. In State of the Art production lines, quality assessment and optimization cycles demand samples to traverse the entire production chain (Fig. 1a), consuming significant resources. By utilizing the DICB as a quality parameter, these cycles are greatly simplified, enabling a quality assessment at one of the initial process stages (Fig. 1b). This approach can be a valuable asset towards conserving resources and may reduce the demand for analytical effort in quality control. Despite that, it has the potential to expedite the development of new materials and recipes. By establishing correlations between slurry, electrode, and cell characteristics, this study offers more detailed insights into the relationship between process, structure, and properties in lithium-ion battery electrodes.

All slurries were processed using a co-rotating twin screw extrusion device, varying the solids content in kneading section. All intermediate products (slurry, electrode and cell) were characterized extensively using optical, physical and electrochemical characterization methods. A general overview of the scope of invesitgation is represented in Fig. 2 .

Since this investigation focuses on CB dispersion and its impact on the resulting intermediary product characteristics (slurry, electrode and cell) in the lithium-ion cathode production, the recipe was kept constant. The recipe comprises 95 wt.-% cathode active material (NCM-622, BASF SE), 3 wt.-% carbon black (Super C65, Imerys Graphite and Carbon), and 2 wt.-% PVDF (PVDF 5130, Solvay). N-Methyl-2-pyrrolidon (NMP electronic grade, BASF SE) serves as the solvent in the dispersion step. During the coating step, the slurry is applied to an aluminum foil current collector (20 μm, 5.4 mg/cm2) with a mass loading of 21 mg/cm2 (3.5 mAh/cm2).

The raw materials, consisting of active material, carbon black and binder, were dry mixed at 49 min−1 for 15 min using a TURBULA® T2F mixer (Willy A. Bachofen AG Maschinenfabrik). The subsequent wet mixing was carried out in a continuous co-rotating twin-screw extrusion device (ZSK 18, Coperion GmbH) with an 18 mm diameter screw and a length of 720 mm. The screw configuration consists of various conveying and kneading elements with a total length of 280 mm conveying elements and 440 mm kneading elements (as exemplified in Fig. 2). Both, the powder mixture and solvent were added using gravimetric dosing units. The powder mixture was introduced in compartment one, while the solvent was added in compartments two and nine. The Dispersion was carried out at a constant screw speed (n = 1200 min−1) and a total mass flow (ṁ = 4.56 kg h−1) with a barrel temperature of T = 30 °C. The final slurry solids content cm was set to 70 wt.-%. Different mixing intensities were achieved by kneading at higher solids contents. In this investigation, the solids content in the kneading section cm,kn (from compartment 2 to 8) was increased from 70 wt.-% to 85 wt.-%.

The slurry was coated onto an aluminum current collector and dried using the continuous roll-to-roll coating system LabCo (Kroenert GmbH & Co KG), featuring a total drying length of 6 m. A comma bar in reverse roll coating mode was selected as the applicator. The coating width was set to 160 mm at 1.5 m min−1. The drying section is divided in three individually controllable segments, with a length of 2 m each. The temperature profile was set to 80 °C / 100 °C / 120 °C at a fan speed of 60 % / 80 % / 100 % (indirect / indirect / direct impingement). All electrodes were densified to a target density of 3.2 g cm−3 using a calander (GKL400, Saueressig) to ensure homogeneous pore structures and maximize the performance in electrochemical characterization.

To assess the influence of CB dispersion on slurry properties, all samples were characterized for particle size and rheological properties. Rheological properties were evaluated using a rotational rheometer (Kinexus Pro+, Netzsch). A shear rate test was performed at 20 °C using a cone-plate geometry (4° cone angle) at a measuring gap of 146 μm. The sample was stressed at increasing shear rates between 0.01 s - 1 and 251.2 s−1. To ensure proper contact between the sample and geometry and remove inner tension, a pre-shearing step was preceded. During pre-shearing a constant shear rate of 0.01 s−1 is applied over a period of two minutes.

The CB particle size of the slurry samples was determined using a laser scattering device (LA-960, HORIBA). Prior to the measurement, all samples were diluted with 24 parts of NMP and centrifuged at 500 rpm for five minutes using a lab centrifuge (Biofuge Primo, Haeraeus). This centrifugation step aimed to separate the denser AM particles from CB particles based on density differences [22]. The measurement results were employed to calculate the Carbon Black Dispersion Index (DICB). The DICB represents a novel method for evaluating bimodal particle size distributions by quantifying the change in surface induced during dispersion. This is realized, by splitting the distribution in two size fractions C1 and C2. Fraction C1 contains particles below the threshold xD = 0.8 μm, representing fine and well dispersed aggregates. The resulting monomodal distribution for fraction C1 can be described using the rel. Amount of particle and the position of the peak represented by the median particle size x50. To quantify the evolution of the peak, the CB dispersion Index DICB is defined as:
D
I CB
=
Q 3
x D
x
50 , C 1

By taking into account geometrical and physical implications for perfect spheres the DICB represents a measure for the increase in CB surface area which is created during dispersion. More detailed information regarding the metric and it's accuracy and application has been provided by Weber et al. [32]. The DICB was utilized to assess the impact of the mixing process on slurry properties and across the properties of intermediate products, namely the electrode and cell.

To investigate the impact of CB dispersion the electrode properties all samples were primarily characterized prior to the calendering step. During calendering the microstructure is rearranged, affecting the CBD. Therefore changes in properties of calendered electrodes cannot be related to the mixing process unrestrictedly. A comprehensive review regarding the impact of calendering has been provided by Abdollahifar et al. [7]. Adhesion strength, conductivity, and porosity were measured. Adhesion strength was measured using a pull-off test as introduced by Haselrieder et al. [33]. It involved separating the coating from the current collector at a 90° angle using double-sided adhesive tape (Tesafix 5696 extrastrong; Tesa). Prior to pull-off a defined pressure of 0.6 MPa is applied for 30 s. The pull-off is performed at a speed of 100 mm min- 1 while measuring the maximum force Fmax. A total of 10 samples was measured for each electrode.

Electrical conductivity was assessed through a two-point approach introduced by Westphal et al. [34]. The test set-up consists of two geometries, with a lower geometry that moved against an upper geometry until a pressure of 0.35 MPa was reached, ensuring even contact and reduced contact resistance. The resistance through the sample was measured while applying 10 mA of current, and specific conductivity was calculated based on resistance and electrode thickness. A total of 10 samples was measured for each electrode. Furthermore, the electrode resistance measurement system RM2610 by Hioki was employed to verify the results, measuring the resistance within the electrode by attaching probes to the electrode surface. The resistivity for each sample was measured prior to the calendering step as well as afterwards.

To gain insight into the electrode microstructure, porosity and pore size distribution were measured using a mercury intrusion porosimeter (PoreMaster 60GT,Quantachrome Instruments). The characterization was performed according to Froboese et al. [35]. Electrode samples of 40 cm2 were characterized under increasing pressure ranging from 76 kPa up to 206.8 MPa, covering pore diameters from 20 to 0.007 μm, according to the Washburn equation.

Cell properties were tested in coin cells. Electrochemical performance was measured in a half-cell setup with lithium metal foil as a counter electrode and a separator by Whatman. The cell was filled with 100 μl of electrolyte consisting of 1 M lithium hexafluorophosphate (LiPF6) in ethylene carbonate/ethyl methyl carbonate (volume ratio of 3:7) with 2 wt% vinylene carbonate. The electrochemical characterization was conducted in a cell tester by Basytec GmbH at a constant temperature of 25 °C. The test procedure included three formation steps, two C-rate tests with 20 cycles at 0.5C CC/CC in between, and relaxation steps at 0.1C (CCCV) charge/discharge. During the test, the C-rate was varied from 0.2 to 3C in constant current (CC) mode in discharge direction. The charge step between each cycle was performed in constant current and constant voltage (CCCV) mode at 0.2C.

To measure ionic resistance using electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), symmetrical cells were constructed with ethylene carbonate/dimethyl carbonate (1:1) and Tetralbutylammonium perchlorate (TBAClO4) as conductive salt. Impedance data was fitted using a parallel resistor and constant phase element (CPE) in series with a finite space warburg (FSW) using a qualified software (RelaxIS 3, rhd instruments). The ionic resistance was determined by extrapolating the low frequency branch to the x-axis in the Nyquist plot, as described by Landesfeind et al. [27]. The difference between the x-axis values obtained from the linear extrapolations corresponds to one third of the ionic resistance of both electrodes. Contrary to the recommendation given by Landesfeind et al., a FSW was used instead of the transmission line model (TLM) to fit the low frequency branch to lower the deviation between fit and measured data. The two elements are exchangeable, since an infinitely long TLM impedance is similar to a Warburg response, which describes a finite length diffusion with a reflective boundary.

The primary aim of the dispersion process for battery electrodes, aside from ensuring the wetting and homogenization of the components, is to strategically adjust the Carbon Black (CB) particles to enhance and optimize the properties of the resulting electrodes. This adjustment is achieved by locally modifying the solids content, denoted as cm,kn. The specific energy input, and by that the shear stress and consequently stress intensity, was significantly increased from initially 125.33 kJ kg−1 (cm,kn = 70 wt.-%) to 367.56 kJ kg−1 (cm,kn = 85 wt.-%). This increase in energy input leads to more intense stress on the CB, resulting in the fracture of agglomerates. Fig. 3 illustrates the measured particle size distributions displaying a bimodal shape, with one peak representing CB aggregates (< 1 μm) and the other one representing the agglomerates (> 1 μm). As the solids content is increased, noticeable changes in CB particle size distribution become apparent. Without a dedicated kneading section, at a constant solids content of 70.00 wt.-%, only a limited degree of fragmentation can be achieved, with just 10 % of CB particles smaller than 1 μm. Once the solids content is raised to 77.50 wt.-%, an almost linear increase in particles below 1 μm is observed. However, once surpassing the 77.50 wt.-% solids content, deagglomeration exponentially increases, peaking at 85.00 wt.-% where nearly 90 % of the CB is deagglomerated.

Similar particle size distributions have been observed in a series of research articles addressing the dispersion of silicon dioxide in a high shear mixer. For instance, Pacek et al. [36] investigated the fragmentation behavior in relation to tip speed and dispersion time, noting a shift from rupture to erosion as the tip speed increased. Rupture entails the fracturing of larger particle clusters into several fragments of similar size, whereas erosion involves stripping significantly smaller fragments (e.g., aggregates) from larger clusters (e.g., agglomerates) [37]. Padron et al. [38] further mentioned that if the applied energy level is exceptionally high, shattering may occur, leading to the fracturing of clusters into numerous small fragments. A similar mechanism was assumed by Kwade et al. [7,39] for the wet comminution in stirred media mills, if high stress intensities are applied. In subsequent research, these three fragmentation mechanisms (shattering, rupture and erosion) were utilized to describe the evolution of particle sizes in correlation with the dispersion process [40–44]. However, it's important to note that the mechanism and efficiency of silicon dioxide fragmentation were found to be primarily influenced by the mixing device and viscosity [41,43,44].

Özcan-Taşkin et al. [42] provided an overview on the evolution of particle size distributions for the different fragmentation mechanisms. Based on this evolution in particle size, the results displayed in Fig. 3 suggest, that the fragmentation of CB in cathode slurries is a combination of rupture and erosion. However, we suggest that there are two different fragmentation mechanisms present:
1.
At lower solids contents (cm,kn < 77.5 wt.-%) the stressing is mainly dominated by rupture, with larger agglomerates primarily rupturing into a few smaller agglomerates. This is visible in the shift of the agglomerate peak between 70 wt% and 73.5 wt%. We propose that this fragmentation mechanism most likely is induced by the screw and occurs in the shear flow. Moreover, we can observe a certain degree of erosion, which is likely induced at the screw tip where high shear stresses are applied to the particles.
2.
Once a certain threshold in solids content is reached, the dispersion mechanism changes drastically. We suggest that the stressing is not majorly induced by the rotating mixing tool (screw) or the shear flow anymore. Moreover the stressing is now a results of particle-particle interactions and the high packing density. The large and rigid NCM particles act as a kind of grinding media, stressing the CB agglomerates. Therefore the stressing is related to the particle movement and the number of stress event, which exponentially increase with increasing solids content cm,kn. This mechanism results in erosion-dominated fragmentation. This shift to an clearly erosion-dominated fragmentation occurs between 77.50 wt.-% and 81.25 wt.-% for the conducted experiments, once the volumetric solids content exceeds 50 vol.-% (see Error! Reference source not found.).

Furthermore, the dispersion mechanism between a twin screw extruder and a dissolver seem to differ vastly. Previous investigation with a similar recipe in a dissolver showed a pronounced overlap of the agglomerate and aggregate peak [32]. This indicates a key difference between stressing of CB in a dissolver and in a twin screw extruder.

By calculating the DICB, the fracturing of CB agglomerates can be quantified by a single value and correlated with the specific energy input (Em). Fig. 4 a displays both the DICB and the viscosity at two different shear rates (0.1 s−1 and 10 s−1), which are plotted against the specific energy input. An approximately sigmoidal increase in DICB is observed with increasing specific energy input, rising from 0.67 μm−1 up to 5.56 μm−1. However, the changes in DICB reflect the changes in fragmentation mechanisms observed in the particle size measurements. At low solids contents, the DICB increases rather linearly with increasing energy input. This represents the conventional stressing of CB in a shear flow. By increasing the solids content the viscosity and therefore shear forces increase. Once the fragmentation mechanism changes to stressing between the active material particles, the DICB increases exponentially in the beginning and ends asymptotically, similar to a comminution process. This rapid increase in DICB once the threshold in solids content cm,kn is exceeded can be explained by the increase in number of stress events. However, comminution processes always approach a finite value, which is when the stress energy and number of stress events are not sufficient to induce further fragmentation. In this case this point is almost reached, since the majority of CB particles (Q3 = 87 %) are fragmented at a DICB of 5.56 μm−1. With a very high amount of stress events the full fragmentation (Q3 = 100 %) of CB could be achieved.

At the same time, the rotational viscosity of the slurry decreases significantly. At a low shear rate (0.1 s−1) the viscosity is reduced to one tenth of the initial value with increasing energy input. Even at higher shear rates (10 s−1) the viscosity decreases by up to 50 %. The progressive fragmentation of CB under higher stress reduces the amount of solvent entrapped in the agglomerate pores, thereby reducing the viscosity, especially at lower shear rates. Similar correlations have been reported and discussed in research [10,15,45].

One explanation for the reduced viscosity at higher shear rates could be that the high stressing affects the binder [46] or particle-binder interactions and therefore the structure of the slurry. The structural change that occurs can be observed in the complex flow behavior of the slurry, which changes with increasing stress (Fig. 4 b). To quantify these changes the phase angle was measured in an amplitude sweep. The results reveal a significant change in the viscoelastic behavior of the slurry, which may also be attributed to CB deagglomeration. With increasing shear stress and stress intensity and thus, CB dispersion, the slurry's flow behavior shifts from being elastically dominated (phase angle below 45°) to free-flowing (phase angle above 45°). However, this transition occurs at relatively low specific energy inputs and thus shear stress and stress intensity, where the breaking mechanism does not seem to be dominated by erosion. Based on these results, it can be expected that the largest particle clusters in combination with the entrapped solvent and the adsorbed PVDF are mainly responsible for the elastic flow behavior. As soon as the largest particle clusters break down into smaller agglomerates, the gelation point (phase angle of 45°) is reached.

Given the significant changes in slurry properties resulting from CB deagglomeration, one can anticipate corresponding alterations in electrode properties. To identify these correlations, the electrode properties are correlated with the DICB. Fig. 5 a illustrates the impact of the CB structure, as represented by DICB, on the specific electrical resistivity (ρ) and the adhesion strength (σn) of the electrodes prior to the calendering step.

The results indicate a substantial improvement in adhesion strength with increasing DICB. Starting at 0.98 MPa for the lowest DICB of 0.67 μm−1, the adhesion strength nearly doubles, reaching 2.05 MPa for a DICB of 5.56 μm−1. This enhancement in adhesion strength can be attributed to the increased particle surface area of CB, which is represented by the rising DICB. This seems to be the results of changes in the CBD. The large agglomerates inside the electrode (DICB = 0.67 μm−1) leads to a coarse meshed CBD. In this case not all particles inside the electrode are well integrated causing pull off at lower forces. With increasing DICB and therefore surface area of CB, the structure of the CBD changes towards a fine meshed CBD, where the majority of particles is well interconnected. The adhesion strength in this study correlates with the CBD formation. The finer the CBD is established the stronger is the interconnection between particles and therefore the adhesion strength.

With the increase in CB surface area, a decrease in the specific resistivity of the electrode is expected due to better developed conductive pathways. The results from the conducted experiments display an asymptotic decrease, starting at 3.97 kΩcm at a DICB of 0.97 μm−1 and declining to 0.64 kΩcm at a DICB of 5.57 μm−1. Simultaneously, the standard deviation decreases, indicating a more uniform distribution of the conductive additive and a fine meshed CBD. However, the electrode with the lowest DICB, as measured at the slurry level, exhibits a relatively low resistivity of 1.87 kΩcm, falling short of expectations to a certain degree. These results were additionally verified with a Hioki resistance measurement system, as shown in Fig. 5 b. Even with the adjustment to a comparable density by calendering, the trend remains unchangend. However the interface resistance is significantly reduced by calendering.

The initial increase in resistivity between a DICB of 0.67 μm−1 and 0.97 μm−1 may result from changes in CB agglomerate size. In context to the particle size measurement results, it is suggested that the relatively low resistivity is achieved by the presence of larger agglomerates (≥ 10 μm). These larger particles occupy up to 10 % of the coating thickness, assuming a spherical shape, and may form electrical long range contacts [8,15] at least locally. It should be noted that particle clusters in this size range are often far from the assumed ideal spherical shape, and even larger particle clusters may exist in the slurry but could be affected by the sample preparation method, rendering them absent in the measured sample. Nevertheless, since the size, shape and numbers of the agglomerates are not represented by DICB, there is no discernible correlation between resistivity and DICB at a low specific energy input.

Apart from the electrical and mechanical properties, changes in electrode structure are expected with varying CB particle size. Fig. 6 a presents the pore size distribution for all samples before densification. The pore sizes are bimodally distributed, with one peak above and one peak below 1 μm. The larger pores (> 1 μm) are positioned between AM particles, and these pores are not directly influenced by changes in DICB. The intruded volume reveals no significant alterations in this section. On the other hand, pore sizes below 1 μm typically represent intraparticular pores within CB agglomerates and aggregates. The intruded volume in this section is a direct result of the CB structure and is notably impacted by the dispersion process.

The pore size distribution displays two noteworthy changes: Firstly, the most significant change is in the secondary peak of the pore size distribution, which contracts and shifts towards smaller pore sizes with increasing DICB (1). The only exception in this case is the electrode with a DICB of 0.68 μm−1, which could be attributed to the differences in CBD structure that also affected the resistivity. However, the impact is minor for the porosity. As CB agglomerates break up, less solvent is entrapped between CB particles, reducing pore formation during the drying step. The pores in CB aggregates are significantly reduced in size, resulting in fewer and smaller pores overall. Additionally, the data suggests a decrease in the intruded volume between pores of 0.3 μm and 1 μm in size (2). With the decreasing presence of agglomerates in the slurry, a denser particle arrangement is achieved when all solvent is evaporated. The larger agglomerates may act as bridges, stabilizing the voids between particles and generating a more porous coating structure. Once these agglomerates are absent, the pore structure is determined by the spherical AM particles, leading to a higher initial density and by that lower theoretical porosity as shown in Fig. 6 b. Similar findings have been reported by Bockholt et al. [15] in context of high intensive dry mixing. By applying high shear forces during dry mixing they fragmented the CB prior to the dispersion process. They observed significant changes in the pore size distribution of the electrodes, which showed the same trend with regards to increasing CB fragmentation. However, in their case an additional dry mixing step with a duration of 30 min was required. These results emphasize the capabilities of a twin screw extrusion device when it comes to dispersion of battery slurries. After calendering all electrodes have a similar porosity around 30 %. Since the electrical resistance within the electrode is reduced in a similar manner for all electrodes after calendering (see Fig. 5 b) no significant impact on the electrical pathways by the initial porosity differences is expected. Additionally, the inner CB porosity εCB is displayed in Fig. 6 b. With increasing DICB and thereby stressing during the dispersion process, CB agglomerates are fractured into aggregates. This change in CB structure is shown by the asymptotic decrease in the CB porosity from 80 % down to 42 % prior to the calendering step. After calendering the inner CB porosity decreases for low DICB. This indicates that larger CB agglomerates are still present within the electrode, which are stressed and eventually fractured or compacted during the calendering process. However, once the majority of CB is fractured during dispersion (DICB ≥ 3.7 μm−1) the calendering process does not affect the CB porosity further. Therefore, the CB porosity does not change after calendering for the DICB of 3.74 μm−1 and 5.56 μm−1. Since most of the difference in porosity are diminished after calendering a significant impact of the pore size on the resulting performance is not expected.

In Fig. 7 a, the specific discharge capacity of the half cells during cell testing is displayed. Additionally, the C-rate of each cycle is denoted in grey blocks behind the data (right y-axis). All half cells exhibit a specific discharge capacity ranging from 163 and 166 mAh/gNCM and show similar performance at low C-rates, with negligible standard deviations. However, electrodes with a higher DICB exhibit a slightly increased specific discharge capacity. When comparing the mean values for the cycles below 1C, the specific capacity for a DICB of 5.56 μm−1 is on average 1–2 % higher than for a DICB of 0.97 μm−1. These changes may also be a result from process inconsistencies.

However, once the C-rate reaches 1C and higher, the specific discharge capacities vary significantly. At 3C, the lowest specific discharge capacity (61.05 mAh g−1) was recorded for the electrode with the highest DICB of 5.56 μm−1, followed by the electrode with the lowest DICB of 0.68 μm−1. Conversely, the highest specific discharge capacity at 3C was attained with a DICB of 3.74 μm−1, indicating an optimum for the CB dispersion.

For a more in-depth analysis of this data, the specific discharge capacity at 1C, 2C, and 3C concerning the DICB as well as the ionic resistance of the electrodes is presented in Fig. 7 b. The initial increase in discharge capacity with increasing DICB can most likely be attributed to the enhanced conductivity of the CBD. With improved conductivity, electron transport at high C-rates, and consequently the specific discharge capacity, improves as well. However, the severe fracture of agglomerates by erosion leads to a decrease in specific capacity. Using a polynomial fit for the discharge capacity (R2 = 0.997 @ 3C) at the optimum is estimated in the range of 2 μm−1 < DICB < 3 μm−1. This particular effect cannot be explained by previously discussed results regarding specific electrical resistance. An optimum in discharge capacity in relation to the CB structure has been reported by Qi et al. [47] and Mayer et al. [14] as well.

Qi et al. [47] examined the impact of CB surface area on C-rate performance of lithium iron phosphate electrodes, using five different types of CB with varying surface areas, ranging from 53 m2g−1 to 580 m2g−1. They observed the highest discharge capacity at 5C using CB with a specific surface area of 180 m2g−1, suggesting that the optimal electrode structure could be achieved using CB with a surface area between 100 and 200 m2g−1. They attributed weaker electrochemical performance when using high surface area CB to insufficient incorporation of CB in the electrode structure, which deteriorates the conductive network and hinders ion transport. Mayer et al. [14] aimed to alter the CB structure in NCM-622 cathodes by varying the tip speed in a planetary mixer and calculated the volumetric mean aggregate particle size of CB agglomerates. They also observed a similar optimum in discharge capacity at 5C.

According to the definition of DICB, the value considers the mean size and amount of the aggregates and can be described as a measure for the total surface area of CB aggregates. By increasing the mixing intensity, we were able to induce a change in surface area, resulting in a similar optimum.

The data of the EIS measurement, displayed in Fig. 7 b on the right y-axis, indicates a minimum in ionic resistivity in the range of 2 μm−1 < DICB < 3 μm−1. These results correlate with the specific discharge capacity observed in half cell testing. To visualize this optimum the data was fitted using a gaussian function (R2 = 0.993). These findings underline that the specific discharge capacity at higher C-rates is mainly limited by ion transport mechanisms rather than electron transport. Furthermore, a direct correlation between CB dispersion and ionic resistivity seems to be evident. An increasing DICB, and thus an improved CB-Binder-network, appears to reduce both electrical resistance and ionic resistivity. However, once a certain threshold is reached, further increase in DICB leads to an exponential increase in ionic resistivity. It is proposed that the substantial increase in CB surface area results in blocked ionic pathways, hindering ion mobility, especially at higher C-rates.

Similar results were reported by Chi et al. [4] when comparing CB, graphene, and a blend of both. They found that the lowest ionic resistance of 15.77 Ωcm−2 and the best rate capability were achieved using a blend of CB and graphene. They attributed this phenomenon to graphene regulating the pore size of the CBD. However, when solely using graphene, the ionic resistance increased. They believed this to be due to graphene covering the active material and decreasing the AM surface area available for electrochemical reactions. In contrast, electrodes using CB as conductive additive showed the highest ionic resistance, which Chi et al. attributed to smaller pores within the CB hindering Li-ion diffusion. Furthermore, Hein et al. [48] simulated the impact of CBD on the lithiation process using different CBD generation algorithms. Using an algorithm that locates the CBD at contact points of the AM leads to severe transport limitations during lithiation.It is anticipated that the initial decrease in ionic resistance in the range of 0.68 μm−1 < DICB < 2.74 μm−1, is a consequence of alterations to the CBD structure and CBD clusters. The presence of poorly fragmented CB agglomerates is likely to result in the formation of a coarse-meshed CB-Binder network. This may result in a heterogeneous distribution of the CBD in the electrode, with some AM particles not being sufficiently connected and the tortuosity increased. This hinders the ion transport out of the electrode. By improving the fragmentation the CBD is homogeneously distributed and a fine meshed CB-binder network can be achieved, providing a good AM connection. By applying even higher stressing during dispersion, similar effects to those observed for graphene [4] might occur. The small CB particles form a thin film on the AM surface. By reducing the surface available for reaction and increasing the length of pathways for Li-ion diffusion, the rate capability is significantly affected. The film formation of CB on the active material is shown in SEM-images in Error! Reference source not found. The CB on the AM surface might also reduce entry points for Li-ions and thereby hinder the intercalation hat higher C-rates. A similar phenomenon was discussed by Gaberscek [49] in relation to lithium iron phosphate particles.

Since the electrode performance as a function of the DICB, in this study varied by increasing solids content in the kneading section, shows an optimal target range of 2.0 μm−1 to 3.0 μm−1, an additional electrode was prepared. The solids content in the extruder kneading section was set to 79.5 wt.-%, resulting in a DICB of 2.74 μm−1. The slurry processing, as well as electrode and cell production, was kept consistent with the experimental section. The highlighted (orange) specific discharge capacity and ionic resistivity within the target range in Fig. 7 b demonstrate an additional improvement of 8 % at 3C, aligning with the increase expected from the data fit.

These results clearly underscore the importance and potential of a customized dispersion process and electrode structure. By utilizing the DICB as a comprehensive parameter, the electrode structure can be optimized concerning both electric and especially ionic pathways, thus maximizing the performance of lithium-ion battery electrodes. A target range for the DICB was defined based on the collected data points for the specific discharge capacity and ionic resistance. Fitting functions were employed to estimate the range for the DICB with improved C-rate capability. The highest discharge capacity at 3C was achieved at DICB = 2.74 μm−1. Nonetheless, the objective of this research article is to provide an exemplar for future optimization processes utilizing the DICB. However, it is acknowledged that the target range and fitting functions may not be universally applicable due to the high complexity and numerous parameters (e.g. recipe, components, mass loading) inherent to electrode production. Nevertheless, in relation to scale-up with a constant set of parameters, this approach has the potential to significantly reduce the experimental effort.

The dispersion process for battery electrodes plays a crucial role in tailoring their properties. This process aims to strategically adjust the Carbon Black (CB) particle size to optimize the resulting electrodes. Our findings shed light on the predominant stress mechanisms, namely rupture and erosion, experienced by CB particles within a twin-screw extruder. As the solids content increased, we observed a shift in fragmentation behavior from rupture dominance to erosion dominance.

To analyze and quantify the variations in CB particle size and their correlation with changes in electrode and cell properties, we employed the Carbon Black Dispersion Index (DICB). The results unveil the profound influence of CB dispersion and the resulting Carbon-Binder-Domain (CBD) on the characteristics of electrodes and cells. By amplifying the mixing intensity in the twin-screw extruder, we could tailor the dispersion progress to our specific needs.

In the context of electrode properties, the study found a strong correlation between DICB and adhesion strength, indicating that higher DICB values improved the mechanical integrity of the electrode. As the DICB increased, the specific electric resistivity decreased, indicating improved electron transport in the electrodes.

Analysis of cell properties demonstrated that electrodes with higher DICB values exhibited improved specific discharge capacity, particularly at higher C-rates. The research revealed that beyond a certain threshold, further increases in DICB resulted in higher ionic resistivity, suggesting that the significant increase in CB surface area blocked ionic pathways, hampering ion mobility at higher C-rates. Results of EIS measurement further support this idea, showing an optimum in ionic resistivity for 2.0 μm- 1 ≤ DICB ≤ 3.0 μm−1.

Additional experiments confirmed the importance of DICB in optimizing electrode performance. An electrode with a DICB in the target range showed an improvement of 26 % in specific discharge capacity at 3C, aligning with the expectations from the data fit. This emphasizes the significance and potential of a customized dispersion process and electrode structure in maximizing the performance of lithium-ion battery electrodes by optimizing both electric and ionic pathways.

In practical terms, the research demonstrated that tailored dispersion processes, guided by the DICB parameter, can significantly impact electrode properties and, by extension, the performance of lithium-ion batteries. These findings underscore the potential for customizing the dispersion process to optimize both electric and ionic pathways, ultimately enhancing the overall efficiency of lithium-ion battery electrodes.

Leveraging the DICB, particularly in industrial applications, yields fresh perspectives on the relationships between product structure and properties. Therefore, this metric can prove invaluable in forthcoming research to estimate electrode and cell attributes immediately following the mixing process. The specific target DICB might vary depending on the chosen material and formulation, and it must be discerned through cell data analysis. Nonetheless, once this target value is established, it can be employed for scaling up operations, facilitating process transfers, and, notably, enhancing quality control procedures.

Marcel Weber: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Jessica Gerstenberg: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Methodology, Formal analysis. Arno Kwade: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare the following financial interests/personal relationships which may be considered as potential competing interests: Arno Kwade reports financial support was provided by Federal Ministry of Education and Research Berlin Office. If there are other authors, they declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the German Federal Ministry for Education and Research (BMBF) and Projektträger Jülich (PTJ) for their support and funding within the project 03XP0343B (IntelliPast),
The authors extend their gratitude to Janos Büsing for the cell assembly.
